executiveSummary:
**Executive Summary: Medicaid Program Performance Analysis**

National Medicaid enrollment continues its post-pandemic normalization trend, with 75.3 million enrollees as of January 2026 showing broad-based declines across major states as redeterminations conclude. Most large states experienced enrollment decreases ranging from 1.8% in New York to 7.6% in Michigan, with only North Carolina bucking this trend at +0.5% growth. Texas mirrors the national pattern with a 3.1% enrollment decline to 4.03 million beneficiaries, maintaining its position as the third-largest state program. Spending patterns from fiscal year 2024 reveal significant per-enrollee cost variations, with the national average at $2,869 per beneficiary. Texas demonstrates relatively efficient spending at $2,988 per enrollee—4% above the national average but substantially below high-cost states like Pennsylvania ($4,377) and Massachusetts ($4,201), while exceeding low-cost states like Florida ($2,031).

Quality performance remains highly variable across states, with national scores averaging 65.6 out of 100 points. Texas ranks 30th nationally with a score of 62, placing it in the moderate performance tier but below the national average and significantly trailing top performers like Massachusetts (81) and Minnesota (80). This quality gap represents a strategic concern given Texas's scale and managed care penetration of 93.3%—well above the national average of 83.2%. Recent federal regulatory activity indicates heightened oversight focus, with temporary enrollment moratoria and enhanced data collection requirements signaling potential policy shifts that could impact state operations and federal matching funds. Leadership should prioritize quality improvement initiatives and monitor federal guidance closely, particularly given Texas's managed care-heavy model and moderate quality performance relative to program size and spending efficiency.
texasSpotlight:
## Texas Medicaid Program Performance Analysis

Texas operates the nation's third-largest Medicaid program with 4.03 million enrollees, serving a substantial portion of its population despite not expanding Medicaid under the ACA. The state's enrollment declined 3.1% year-over-year, tracking closely with national unwinding trends and performing better than several peer states including Illinois (-6.2%) and Michigan (-7.6%), though lagging behind North Carolina's modest growth (+0.5%). At $2,988 per enrollee, Texas spends above the national average of $2,869 but remains significantly below expansion states like Pennsylvania ($4,377) and Ohio ($3,561), reflecting both its constrained eligibility criteria and potentially different service mix. The state's robust managed care penetration of 93.3% substantially exceeds the national average of 83.2%, indicating a mature, well-established delivery system that covers nearly all beneficiaries through coordinated care arrangements.

However, Texas faces notable quality challenges that warrant attention. With a quality score of 62/100, the state ranks 30th nationally and falls into the "moderate" performance tier—a concerning position for such a large program. This places Texas well below high-performing states like Massachusetts (81) and Minnesota (80), though above the lowest-performing southern peers like Arkansas (54) and Mississippi (48). The moderate quality performance, combined with the state's non-expansion status, suggests potential gaps in care coordination and outcomes that could impact the 4+ million Texans relying on the program. Areas to monitor include whether the strong managed care infrastructure can drive quality improvements, how ongoing federal regulatory changes around enrollment moratoria might affect program operations, and whether Texas's cost-effective spending model can be enhanced to deliver better health outcomes without compromising fiscal sustainability.

alerts.title: Pennsylvania Per-Enrollee Spending 53% Above National Average
alerts.description: Pennsylvania's per-enrollee spending of $4,377 is significantly higher than the national average of $2,869, indicating potential cost control issues.

alerts.title: Five States Show Critically Low Quality Performance
alerts.description: Arkansas, South Carolina, Alabama, Oklahoma, and Mississippi all scored below 55 on quality measures, well below the national average of 65.6.

alerts.title: Florida Shows High Efficiency with Low Per-Enrollee Costs
alerts.description: Florida maintains the lowest per-enrollee spending at $2,031 while serving 3.6 million enrollees, demonstrating potential cost management best practices.

alerts.title: Texas Shows Moderate Quality Despite High Managed Care Use
alerts.description: Texas has 93.3% managed care penetration (above 83.2% national average) but only ranks 30th in quality scores, suggesting managed care effectiveness issues.
alerts.sourceLabel: CMS Quality and Managed Care Data

alerts.title: Massachusetts Achieves Top Quality with High Investment
alerts.description: Massachusetts leads quality scores at 81/100 with correspondingly high per-enrollee spending of $4,201, demonstrating potential value-based care success.

executiveInsights.title: Texas Medicaid enrollment declining 3.1% while maintaining massive scale
executiveInsights.summary: Texas has 4.03M Medicaid enrollees with a -3.1% YoY decline, representing the third-largest state program nationally. This decline mirrors national unwinding trends but creates program stability challenges.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Enrollment declines directly impact contract volumes and revenue projections for Accenture's delivery model. HHSC needs immediate visibility into whether this trend will continue affecting budget planning.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Flag enrollment trend analysis to HHSC leadership for Q2 planning

executiveInsights.title: Texas quality score of 62/100 ranks #30 nationally, below high performers
executiveInsights.summary: Texas scores 62/100 on quality measures, ranking 30th out of 50 states in moderate tier. Top performers like MA (81) and MN (80) significantly outperform Texas.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Quality performance directly impacts federal funding, CMS oversight intensity, and potential contract penalties. This creates immediate risk for both HHSC and Accenture's performance metrics.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review quality improvement initiatives with HHSC quality team immediately

executiveInsights.title: Federal moratorium signals impacting Medicaid enrollment processes
executiveInsights.summary: Multiple Federal Register notices announce nationwide temporary moratoriums on Medicare and Medicaid program enrollment. These regulatory changes are actively being implemented in 2026.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Enrollment moratoriums could disrupt current system processes and require immediate system configuration changes that Accenture manages for HHSC.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Flag Federal Register moratorium notices to HHSC compliance team

executiveInsights.title: Texas managed care penetration at 93.3% exceeds national average of 83.2%
executiveInsights.summary: Texas achieves 93.3% managed care penetration with 3.86M managed care enrollees, significantly above the 83.2% national average. This represents strong program maturity.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: High managed care penetration indicates sophisticated program operations that require advanced technical capabilities from Accenture's platform and potentially higher contract complexity.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review managed care system capacity with technical delivery leads

executiveInsights.title: Texas per-enrollee costs of $2,988 track 4% above national average
executiveInsights.summary: Texas spends $2,988 per enrollee compared to the national average of $2,869, with total expenditures of $12.05B. This represents a $119 per-member premium above national benchmark.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Higher per-enrollee costs indicate either more complex member populations or operational inefficiencies that HHSC and Accenture should investigate for potential cost management opportunities.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review cost drivers analysis with HHSC finance team

riskOpportunity.label: Enrollment decline acceleration
riskOpportunity.description: Texas Medicaid enrollment dropping 3.1% YoY could accelerate, reducing program scope and contract values.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop cost optimization and efficiency solutions to maintain contract value despite lower enrollment.

riskOpportunity.label: Quality improvement initiative
riskOpportunity.description: Texas ranks #30 with 62/100 quality score, creating demand for performance enhancement services.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Propose comprehensive quality improvement program targeting specific low-performing metrics.

riskOpportunity.label: Federal oversight intensification
riskOpportunity.description: Multiple HHS OIG investigations and enrollment moratoria signal increased federal scrutiny of Medicaid operations.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Strengthen compliance monitoring and documentation processes across all Texas HHSC engagements.

riskOpportunity.label: Managed care recompete
riskOpportunity.description: High 93.3% managed care penetration in Texas creates significant procurement opportunities as contracts renew.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Position for upcoming managed care organization contract renewals with enhanced technology solutions.

riskOpportunity.label: Cost efficiency pressure
riskOpportunity.description: Texas per-enrollee costs at $2,988 are above national average, creating pressure for cost reduction initiatives.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop analytics-driven cost containment strategies to demonstrate measurable savings.

riskOpportunity.label: Digital transformation gap
riskOpportunity.description: Upcoming CMS information collection requirements may expose technology gaps requiring modernization investments.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Accelerate digital health platform capabilities to meet evolving CMS reporting requirements.

riskOpportunity.label: Competitive displacement risk
riskOpportunity.description: Active procurement environment in Texas creates risk of competitor displacement of existing Accenture positions.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Strengthen incumbent positioning through proactive relationship management and performance excellence.
